optimizer.title: Campaign optimizer
optimizer.p1: Shows campaigns joined to pre-aggregated campaign performance data from the 'Entity relationships' section.
optimizer.p2: Demonstrates the following advanced table features:

p2_list.item_2: Inline updates (of Active and Daily budget)
p2_list.item_3: Bulk updates via row selection. Selection respects filters after update. Uses browser notifications if allowed
p2_list.item_4: Totals row which respects filtering and column selection and ignores paging
p2_list.item_5: Row expansion showing latest daily performance metrics

mcp_server.p1: The backend contains an {MCPServer} which allows LLMs to query the campaign performance data shown in the "Entity relationships" section above.
mcp_server.p2: Currently there is no attached LLM model, so the natural language to MCP tool translation is simulated. However the MCP server query and response handling is fully functional.

campaign_perf.title: Data entity: campaign performance
campaign_perf.p1: Data entities show primary columns of parent entities and should have no child entities.
campaign_perf.p2: Data is usually managed by background processes and not editable via API or UI.

campaigns.title: Regular entity: campaigns
campaigns.p1: Regular entities show primary columns of parent entities and aggregates of child entities.
campaigns.p2: In this case, we show the name column of the parent country and vertical tables, and the date bounds of the child campaign performance table.
campaigns.p3: The parent entities should be filterable.

verticals.title: Parent entity: verticals
verticals.p1: Parent entities show aggregates (e.g. count) of child entities. In this case, campaign is the child entity.
verticals.p2: If needed, provide a quick way to filter child entities by the parent, as shown with the "View campaigns" {viewCampsIcon} button below.
verticals.p3: Disable deletion of the parent if it has associated child entities, unless there is a composition relationship which allows cascaded deletion.

campaign_charts.p1: Using the campaign data from the 'Entity relationships' section, demonstrates further chart types.
campaign_charts.p2: The title and label text colors in the chart library have been overriden to respect the application-level light or dark mode.
campaign_charts.spend_vs_revenue: Campaign spend vs revenue: last 7 days

xr_performance.title: Exchange rate performance
xr_performance.p1: Using the exchange rates from the 'External data' section, this chart shows normalized EUR performance vs active currencies for the selected period.
xr_performance.p2: The data point tooltips show the actual exchange rate rather than the normalized value.

currencies.p1: Currency code and name are synced from the European Central Bank API.
currencies.p2: Active and symbol are user-maintained metadata fields, stored independently of the synced data.

exchange_rates.p1: This data is synced from the European Central Bank API. The sync methods in the backend demonstrate high-volume data processing.
exchange_rates.p2: When working with large result sets, the total number of results shown in the pagination footer is estimated using database statistics, as indicated by the "~" prefix.

geo_ip.title: Geo IP information
geo_ip.p1: Geo IP data is imported from MaxMind GeoLite2-City CSV files. In the backend, this demonstrates streaming of large files into the database.

advanced_tables.title: Advanced tables
advanced_tables.subtitle: Complex data presentation.
advanced_tables.p1: This section demonstrates some of the more advanced features of Vuetify's data tables.

ai.title: AI / LLMs
ai.subtitle: Adding LLM tools.
ai.p1: This section shows how to add custom data sources to LLMs.

charts.title: Charts
charts.subtitle: Visualizing data with charts.
charts.p1: This section shows some types of charts using Chart.js.

entity_relationships.title: Entity relationships
entity_relationships.subtitle: Managing relationships between entities.
entity_relationships.p1: This section examines the implications of an entity hierarchy: parent, regular, and child/data entities.

external_data.title: External data
external_data.subtitle: Importing and managing external data sources.
external_data.p1: This section shows data from the European Central Bank's API and the MaxMind API.
external_data.p2: In the backend, the it shows how to merge data efficiently using hash tables and how to stream from file directly into the database.

parallel_processing.title: Parallel processing
parallel_processing.subtitle: Improved data throughput.
parallel_processing.p1: This section shows an implementation of job workflows, where each step is a fake backend command to manipulate data.
parallel_processing.p2: When a job is started, each step that can be executed in parallel runs concurrently, and structures are in place to manage failure gracefully.

project_stack.title: Project stack and repositories
project_stack.subtitle: Modern technologies with custom open source libraries on top.
project_stack.p1: This project uses {PostgreSQL} for the database, {Go} on the server, and {VueJs} with {Vuetify} components in the UI.
project_stack.p2: The LoveYourStack open source repositories relevant to this project are:

p2_list.item_3: {connectors} for the ECB and MaxMind connectors.
p2_list.item_4: {lysVue} for the UI library, which is available via {npm}.

project_stack.p3: The cards below give an overview of the subheadings in the left and right navigation panels.

saas.title: SaaS / multi-tenancy
saas.subtitle: Solving SaaS on the data level.
saas.p1: This section shows how to use Postgres RLS (Row-Level Security) to implement multi-tenancy in a SaaS application.
saas.p2: It shows how to access the data both from an internal database connection, and also from a tenant application using a special RLS-enabled database connection.

server_push.title: Server push
server_push.subtitle: Real-time notifications and updates.
server_push.p1: This section demonstrates how data is pushed from database to app (via listen/notify) and from app to frontend (via websockets).

type_handling.title: Type handling
type_handling.subtitle: Dealing with different data types.
type_handling.p1: This section gets the basics out the way; it demonstrates how to handle common data types (e.g. strings, numbers, dates, arrays).
type_handling.p2: It examines the implications of mandatory vs optional vs default values.

user_data_retention.title: User data retention
user_data_retention.subtitle: Techniques for preserving user data.
user_data_retention.p1: This section demonstrates how to properly archive (soft delete) data including cascading effects, and how to track user changes using a database audit log.

flows.p1: A flow is a set of connected processes. Click 'View steps' on one of the flows to see more information.

steps.title: Steps
steps.p1: Each card below is a step in the process with an associated fake backend command. On large screens: steps on the right depend on steps to their left.
steps.p2: Each step can be run independently or with its dependencies. Steps without dependencies are run in parallel. When you run a step, a page opens to display its progress and results.
steps.p3: Demonstrates even spacing of hierarchical data. In the backend, demonstrates:

p3_list.item_1: Recursive relationships (steps can depend on other steps)
p3_list.item_2: Parallel processing, with the option to cancel on the first error or continue

runs.p1: Each run is an execution of a flow step and its dependencies. Click 'View steps' to see the result of each process step.

points.p1: Each card below shows the result of the execution of a process step.
points.p2: The cards are updated automatically every 2 seconds as long as there is at least one process still running.
points.p3: Each fake running process has a 5% chance per second of failing.

internal_view.p1: This example is based on the classic Northwind database.
internal_view.p2: We are a food trader, and we have suppliers. The supplier employees can log in to manage their products.
internal_view.p3: This page shows supplier employees and products as we would see them in our internal application.
internal_view.p4: The employees table allows a fake login of a supplier employee in order to show data in the Tenant view. The logged-in employee's email is highlighted in bold.

supplier_employees.p1: Internal view: all supplier employees are visible and editable.

supplier_products.p1: Internal view: all supplier products are visible but not editable.

tenant_view.p1: This page shows supplier employees and products as the logged-in supplier employee would see them.
tenant_view.p2: Suppliers have a separate backend API (and normally a separate UI) so that endpoints and deployments are separated from the internal application.
tenant_view.p3: Tenant isolation (i.e. each supplier can only see his own data) is enforced using database row-level security.
tenant_view.please_login: Please login as an employee on the Internal view page.

supplier_employees.p1: Tenant view: only employees of the tenant are visible, and are not editable.

supplier_products.p1: Tenant view: only products of the tenant are visible, and are editable by the tenant.

notifications.p1: Notifications are added in the database so they are persistent and can be used by any component. They are pushed to the app using listen/notify, and from the app to the frontend via websockets.
notifications.p2: If you click one of the buttons below, a notification will be added and the bell icon in the top-right will indicate an unread notification. You can click the icon to read the notification as normal.
notifications.p3: If you allow browser notifications for this site, you will also see a pop-up notification.

arrays.p1: In table, join elements if space allows. In form, split elements by newline to simplify editing.

default_values.p1: Differentiate between true (mandatory) defaults and defaults in the sense of a suggested value.

mandatory_values.p1: All columns require a value. Text may not be empty (''). This table also demonstrates filter functionality per type.
mandatory_values.p2: Some further points which apply to all tables in this app:

p2_list.item_1: The page content and the table paging footers are aligned to the left. This allows smooth paging even if the content width changes
p2_list.item_2: All tables allow multi-column sorting by clicking on the column headers
p2_list.item_3: Selected filtering and sorting options are remembered for each table. They can be reset by choosing "Reset table" from the menu in the top right corner
p2_list.item_4: Columns can be hidden on smaller screens as required by using the "Adjust columns" feature from the table menu
p2_list.item_5: Data can be downloaded to Excel from the table menu. The downloaded data respects the current filters

optional_values.p1: All columns are optional. Zero-values by type are inserted where no value is given.

variants.p1: Common variants of basic data types.

authors.p1: The Edit form has no delete option; authors and their books are instead archived to separate storage.
authors.p2: Archived authors can be shown by selecting "Show archive" from the menu on the right.
authors.p3: When the archive is shown, a button {restoreIcon} to restore archived authors to the main table is available.
authors.p4: The Show update history button {showUpdateHistIcon} shows the data changes, if any, for each author.

books.p1: If the author is archived, his active books will also be automatically archived.
books.p2: However, books by active authors can also be archived separately. Restoration is only possible if the book was archived separately.
